abstract:
Supervised machine learning typically involves classifying new data points based on a set of previously labeled training examples. In this paper, we demonstrate that the support vector machine (SVM)—a cornerstone of optimized binary classification—can be implemented on a quantum computer with a dramatic reduction in complexity. Specifically, our quantum SVM achieves runtimes that scale logarithmically with both the dimension of the feature vectors and the total number of training examples. 

By achieving this logarithmic scaling, we demonstrate an exponential speedup over classical sampling algorithms in scenarios where they would otherwise require polynomial time. The engine behind this "quantum big data" algorithm is a specialized nonsparse matrix exponentiation technique. This method allows for the efficient execution of matrix inversion on the training data’s inner-product (kernel) matrix, effectively translating high-dimensional optimization problems into a manageable quantum framework.
